unverified.label: RL Breaks the IID Assumption
unverified.quote: we're not talking about IID data here... the agent is actually influencing the data that it sees this is like an active learning process

unverified.label: Zero-Knowledge Self-Play Loop (AlphaZero)

all_results.label: The Era of Experience Over Human Data
all_results.quote: human data, the era of human data in itself leads to AI systems that can't in isolation go all the way to super intelligence. Why is that? Well, it's because they can't discover new knowledge.

all_results.label: Tabula Rasa Learning Surpasses Human Expertise
all_results.quote: Our results comprehensively demonstrate that a pure reinforcement learning approach is fully feasible, even in the most challenging of domains: it is possible to train to superhuman level, without human examples or guidance, given no knowledge of the domain beyond basic rules.

all_results.label: The Purity of Self-Learning (The Bitter Lesson)
all_results.quote: If you throw out the human data, you actually spend more effort on how the system can learn for itself. And that's the part which can then learn and learn and learn forever.

all_results.label: Generality Over Domain Specificity
all_results.quote: ...a general-purpose reinforcement learning algorithm can achieve, tabula rasa, superhuman performance across many challenging domains.

all_results.label: RLHF is Fundamentally Ungrounded
all_results.quote: I want to say that when we train a system from human feedback that it is not grounded... it's like the human is prejudging the output of the system.

all_results.label: The Beauty of Diverse Intelligence
all_results.quote: why not build systems which are doing things which humans can't do, which are, you know, actually occupying a different niche in the space of intelligence and have their own strengths and and and weaknesses.

all_results.label: Autonomous Algorithm Discovery
all_results.quote: Unlike humans, whose learning mechanism has been naturally discovered by biological evolution, RL algorithms are typically manually designed. This is usually slow and laborious, and limited by reliance on human knowledge and intuition.

all_results.label: Big Worlds as Occam's Razor
all_results.quote: In contrast, big worlds act as a form of Occam’s razor, with only the simplest and clearest ideas achieving success.

all_results.label: Creativity is Trial-and-Error Learning
all_results.quote: The whole process of trial-and-error learning... is a process of creativity. ... The process is a million mini discoveries, one after the other. It is the essence of creativity.

all_results.label: Deterministic Policy Gradients Efficiency
all_results.quote: In the stochastic case, the policy gradient integrates over both state and action spaces, whereas in the deterministic case it only integrates over the state space.

all_results.label: Glorious Failure Over Incremental Success
all_results.quote: it's much better, in my opinion, to, try and do something glorious in research, and maybe fail, than it is to do something you know is incremental, and be guaranteed to succeed.

all_results.label: Intelligence Formalized as Reinforcement Learning
all_results.quote: I see the problem of intelligence. I would say it can be formalised as the reinforcement learning problem and that that formalization is enough to capture most, if not all, of the things that we mean by intelligence

all_results.label: Limitations of Model-Based RL
all_results.quote: Model-based RL is only as good as the estimated model

all_results.label: MCTS Scales Better Than Alpha-Beta
all_results.quote: AlphaZero’s MCTS scaled more effectively with thinking time than either Stockfish or Elmo, calling into question the widely held belief that alpha-beta search is inherently superior in these domains.

all_results.label: RL Breaks the IID Assumption
all_results.quote: we're not talking about IID data here... the agent is actually influencing the data that it sees this is like an active learning process

all_results.label: Simple Ideas Generalize Best
all_results.quote: I think it's likely to be the case that it's the simple, clear ideas which will have the longest legs and which will carry us further into the future.

all_results.label: Stochastic Policies for State Aliasing
all_results.quote: Whenever state aliasing occurs a stochastic policy can do better than a deterministic policy

all_results.label: Temporality and Local Evaluation
all_results.quote: Rather than trying to approximate the entire state space, our idea is to specialise the evaluation function to the current region of the state space.

all_results.label: The Markov Property for State
all_results.quote: if you have the mark of property then the future is independent of the past given the present in other words that you only need to store this St here... you can throw away the whole rest of the history

all_results.label: The Reward Hypothesis
all_results.quote: all goals can be described by the maximization of expected cumulative reward.

all_results.label: Zero-Knowledge Self-Play Loop (AlphaZero)
all_results.quote: you take the best move according to your search and you train your policy to do more things like that... and you train your value function based on how the game actually panned out... You just iterate that millions of times and out pops a superhuman game player.

all_results.label: AlphaZero Self-Play Reinforcement Learning
all_results.quote: The main idea of our reinforcement learning algorithm is to use these search operators repeatedly in a policy iteration procedure: the neural network’s parameters are updated to make the move probabilities and value more closely match the improved search probabilities and self-play winner.

all_results.label: The Rising Tide Problem Selection
all_results.quote: picking a good problem is about picking something which is just the right height above the tide that you know it's it's feasible like it's going to be cracked within the next you know a couple of years.

all_results.label: Autonomous RL Rule Discovery (DiscoRL)
all_results.quote: During the discovery process, we instantiate a population of agents, each of which interacts with its own instance of an environment taken from a diverse set of challenging tasks... We then use the meta-gradient method to incrementally improve the RL rule.

all_results.label: Compatible Off-Policy Deterministic Actor-Critic (COPDAC)
all_results.quote: The basic idea is to choose actions according to a stochastic behaviour policy (to ensure adequate exploration), but to learn about a deterministic target policy (exploiting the efficiency of the deterministic policy gradient).

all_results.label: Deep Q-Networks (DQN)
all_results.quote: DQN uses experience replay and fixed Q-targets

all_results.label: Dyna-2 Architecture
all_results.quote: The temporal-difference learning algorithm acquires general domain knowledge from its past interactions with the world. In contrast, the temporal-difference search algorithm acquires local knowledge that is specialised to the current state... We combine both forms of knowledge together in the Dyna-2 architecture.

all_results.quote: in RL the environment is unknown in planning the environment is known

all_results.label: "Human-like" Selective Search

all_results.quote: Unlike humans, whose learning mechanism has been naturally discovered by biological evolution, RL algorithms are typically manually designed.
